title: Profit for the biggest U.S. oil companies declined in 1st quarter – but only on paper
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: In the first quarter, U.S. oil giants Exxon Mobil and Chevron saw paper profit declines due to failed financial hedges after attacks on Iran. Despite this, thei
excerpt: In the first quarter, U.S. oil giants Exxon Mobil and Chevron saw paper profit declines due to failed financial hedges after attacks on Iran. Despite this, their actual profits exceeded expectations. Shares rose as market volatility eased.

content.introduction: In the first quarter, U.S. oil giants Exxon Mobil and Chevron saw paper profit declines due to failed financial hedges after attacks on Iran. Despite this, their actual profits exceeded expectations. Shares rose as market volatility eased.
body: # Profit for the biggest US oil companies declined in the first quarter, but only on paper. Exxon Mobil and Chevron reported quarterly results on Friday and the shares of both companies, up sharply this week, ticked higher before the opening bell. In this photo taken with a slow shutter speed, traffic moves past a sign for a Mobil gas station on Wednesday, April 29, 2026, in Portland, Ore. Profit for the two largest oil companies in the U.S. tumbled during the first quarter, a three-month period in which the price of crude and gasoline rocketed higher. It’s a setback on paper only, however, the result of financial hedges that backfired after the U.S. and Israel launched attacks on Iran in late February. Exxon Mobil and Chevron reported quarterly results on Friday, with adjusted profits for both companies topping Wall Street expectations. Chevron reported a first-quarter profit of $2.21 billion, or $1.11 per share.

## Share your email and we’ll share the latest news. # Profit for the biggest U.S. oil companies declined in 1st quarter – but only on paper. Profit for the two largest oil companies in the U.S. tumbled during the first quarter, a three-month period in which the price of crude and gasoline rocketed higher. It's a setback on paper only, however, the result of financial hedges that backfired after the U.S. and Israel launched attacks on Iran in late February. With energy prices depressed at the start of the year, Exxon Mobil and Chevron had arranged hedges to offset volatility, a standard practice in the industry. In the aftermath of an attack by the U.S. and Israel on Iran, however, the physical delivery of oil became impossible with the Strait of Hormuz essentially closed. Chevron reported a first-quarter profit of $2.21 billion, or $1.11 per share.
